retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Papanicolaou Smear -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Pap smears are the screening tool to assess cellular changes within the cervix. Documentation exists for varying levels of sensitivity and specificity for the test. [4] Because the test is not 100% sensitive, it is important to conduct pap smears routinely. [5] [6] From a societal lens, there are racial disparities in the timeliness of cervical cancer diagnosis. The localized diagnosis of cervical cancer is 47% in White and 36% in Black individuals. The regionalized diagnosis of cervical cancer is 35% in White and 41% in Black populations. [3] There is a decrease in the early detection of cervical cancer in Black women, which results in detection when the disease has further progressed.

retrieved_docs.title: Preventing Cervical Cancer: Best Practices in Pap and HPV Testing -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Bethesda System Report
retrieved_docs.content: Specimen type: Indicates whether the sample is conventional or liquid-based Specimen adequacy: Indicates whether the specimen is satisfactory or unsatisfactory for evaluation Any smear with abnormal cells is, by definition, satisfactory for evaluation. Presence of endocervical or transformation zone is an indicator of specimen adequacy, but is not required for specimen adequacy (see Image. Pap with Endocervical Cells). Specimens may be considered unsatisfactory for evaluation because too few cells are collected; cervical cells are obscured by mucus, inflammation, lubricants, and inappropriate fixation. General categorization: Indicates general findings Negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy Epithelial cell abnormality (concerning abnormal squamous or glandular epithelial interpretation or result) Other (concerning abnormal interpretation or result, including organisms or other non-neoplastic findings)

retrieved_docs.content: The success and utility of Pap smears can be demonstrated through the expansion of exfoliative cytopathology and cytological fluid analysis as a reliable technique to detect cancers and pre-cancers throughout the body, including the thyroid, lungs, bladder, and gastrointestinal tract. [19] [62] [ NIH. Clinical Methods: The History, Physical, and Laboratory Examinations. 3rd edition ]

retrieved_docs.title: Preventing Cervical Cancer: Best Practices in Pap and HPV Testing -- Quality Control and Lab Safety -- Quality of Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: Automated, computer-assisted, and artificial intelligence–assisted cytopathological analysis is a growing area of research. Computer-assisted interpretation of cytology has been successfully attempted since the late 1990s. As of 1997, 2 systems have received regulatory approval by the FDA, including Papnet, a computerized tool that uses neural-network technology to identify slides initially read as negative to be rescreened due to likelihood of containing abnormal cells, and AutoPap, which uses an algorithm-based technology to identify slides likely to contain abnormal cells in contrast to random rescreening. [68]

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Papanicolaou Smear -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical cancer screening has become one of the cornerstones of preventative screening in the gynecological world. George Papanicolaou invented this screening event, hence the name "Pap" smear, and was first used in 1943. The test collects cervical cells near the cervical transitional (transformation) zone and determines if any of them have precancerous changes. Since 1943, few classification systems have been put in place to detail the different types of collected cells; the current system followed in the United States is the 2014 Bethesda System for Reporting Cervical Cytology. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Screening(Archived) -- Potential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, screening results are reported using the Bethesda System of cervical cytology. Reports include specimen type, specimen adequacy, interpretation, adjunctive testing, and computer-assisted interpretation of cervical cytology. [6] The specimen type will define the method of collection used. Specimen adequacy will notify the clinician if the specimen was satisfactory for evaluation or if the specimen was rejected and not processed. Reports will also describe the presence or absence of the transformation zone and whether the clinician will need to repeat the specimen collection. If the specimen analysis was by an automated device, the device must be included in the report.

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Papanicolaou Smear -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: LSIL : Repeat Pap smear in 1 year.

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Papanicolaou Smear -- Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: Liquid-based cytology preserves the cells in a buffered alcoholic solution. It has been suggested that liquid-based can better identify glandular pathology. The conventional cytology method directly transfers the cells collected from the brush onto a slide. This transfer is why this technique is called a pap smear. When transferring the specimen onto the slide, it is important to minimize drying artifacts due to prolonged contact with the air. According to the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG), both the liquid-based and conventional techniques are equally effective at screening for cervical cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Preventing Cervical Cancer: Best Practices in Pap and HPV Testing -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Computer-assisted interpretation of cervical cytology: If the patient is examined using an automated device, the report should specify the device and the result Educational notes and comments: Provide optional suggestions that should be concise and consistent with clinical follow-up guidelines published by professional organizations Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Preventing Cervical Cancer: Best Practices in Pap and HPV Testing -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Advantages of liquid-based cytology include rinsing specimens to remove obscuring cellular material and debris, and more evenly distributing diagnostically relevant cells in a single layer. In addition, residual fluid can be reserved for further investigations, such as HPV testing, sexually transmitted infection testing, and repeat smears. [39] [41] However, liquid-based cytology is more expensive, both in terms of capital investment and ongoing operating costs, which compromises its utility in lower-resource settings. [39] [41] In addition, comparative analyses with respect to sensitivity and specificity of the 2 methods have yielded conflicting results, especially with respect to higher-grade lesions. [41] [ AAFP. Conventional Pap Smear vs. Liquid-Based Cytology ] Current evidence, according to the 2018 USPSTF practice guidelines, does not denote any clinically important cytopathological differences. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Papanicolaou Smear -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance , ASC-US: This management plan depends on the age of the patient. For patients between 21 and 24, repeat Pap smear in 1 year if the status of the HPV is positive or unknown. It is also acceptable to perform a reflex HPV test on the same pap smear sample. HPV status will determine future pap smear frequencies. If HPV is negative, then the patient is recommended for follow-up in 3 years. If the follow-up Pap smear continues to show ASC-US, then an additional Pap smear in 1 year is recommended. For patients between 25 and 29, screening is still the same if they have negative HPV. However, if they have positive HPV, colposcopy is recommended instead of waiting one year for a follow-up pap smear. If at the follow-up pap smear, ASC-US is still present, the next step is colposcopy. For patients between 30 to 65 years of age, there is a more significant concern for the progression of cervical cancer. If ASC-US is documented on a pap smear and they are HPV negative, they will follow the same process as the other age groups. If HPV status is positive or unknown, then a repeat pap smear is recommended within four months.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Squamous Cells of Undetermined Significance -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Women who have ever been sexually active are at risk of developing cervical cancer; however, there are women with a greater risk profile than others. Risk factors commonly associated with the development of cervical cancer include younger age at sexual debut, multiple concurrent sexual partners, chronic intense smoking, HIV infection, and persistent infection with hrHPV. Although the Pap test dates back to the late 1940s, it has not been fully adopted and used in resource-limited settings for several reasons, including its high cost and delays in obtaining results. Very low rates of Pap smears have been reported in resource-limited countries, including Jamaica at 15% and Nicaragua at 20% national coverage. In some Asian and African countries, Pap testing rates are even lower compared to that in Jamaica, or Pap testing does not even exist. [21] In other regions, when cervical screening programs were initially implemented, opportunistic Pap smear tests were used, but the coverage rates were still notably low. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Preventing Cervical Cancer: Best Practices in Pap and HPV Testing -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Liquid-based cytology was introduced in 1996 as an evolution of conventional Pap smears to improve their diagnostic reliability. [5] [39] [40] [ AAFP. Conventional Pap Smear vs. Liquid-Based Cytology ] As of 2022, several commercial systems have been developed, including 2 FDA-approved systems in the United States—ThinPrep, first approved in 1996, and SurePath, first approved in 1999.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2403
retrieved_docs.content: Other pregnancy and delivery including normal conditions are clinically associated with an abnormal cervical smear (لطاخة عنق الرحم غير طبيعية). This relationship indicates that cervical cytology abnormalities can occur in the setting of pregnancy and delivery, even when the obstetric course is normal, underscoring the importance of appropriate cervical screening and follow‑up during prenatal and postpartum care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2162
retrieved_docs.content: Endocervical biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on the cervix are both categorized as “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” indicating that they belong to the same group of specialized examinations used to assess cervical pathology within the female reproductive system. This classification underscores their role as ancillary diagnostic tools in gynecologic practice, employed when routine screening or imaging suggests the need for tissue sampling or further evaluation of cervical abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “حمل منتبذ” (abnormal pregnancy) is clinically associated with “لطاخة عنق الرحم غير طبيعية” (abnormal cervical smear), and the abnormal cervical smear (“لطاخة عنق الرحم غير طبيعية”) is in turn clinically associated with “مشيمة منزاحة” (placenta previa). This interrelationship suggests that patients presenting with an abnormal pregnancy may also exhibit abnormal cervical cytology, and that abnormal cervical cytology may be linked to the presence of a low‑lying placenta, underscoring the importance of comprehensive obstetric and gynecologic evaluation in such cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1579
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial sampling (biopsy) with or without endocervical sampling (biopsy), without cervical dilation, any method (separate procedure) is a diagnostic procedure that falls within the specialty of physicians practicing obstetrics and gynecology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “لطاخة عنق الرحم غير طبيعية” (an abnormal cervical smear) is clinically associated with “مشيمة منزاحة” (placenta previa). This association indicates that women who present with abnormal cervical cytology may have an increased likelihood of developing a low‑lying placenta, a relationship that is important for obstetric risk assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Other male genital disorders are considered a subcategory of prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and are linked to the practice of screening (التحصّي). Recognizing that other male genital disorders fall under the broader category of prostate disorder and are associated with screening emphasizes the importance of appropriate diagnostic evaluation in the management of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2486
retrieved_docs.content: Endocervical biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on the cervix are both classified as “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs.” This category of female‑organ diagnostic procedures is itself classified under the broader modality of pelvic endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض). Consequently, endocervical biopsy and similar cervical diagnostic techniques are understood to be specialized forms of pelvic endoscopic procedures used in the evaluation of female patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 565
retrieved_docs.content: reflecting combined surgical management of gynecologic malignancies. Cervical precancerous lesions, including cervical cancer CIN grades and cervical cancer in situ CIN III, are clinically associated with abdominal hysterectomy, underscoring its relevance in treating high‑grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia. Additionally, an increased head circumference (حج القحف) is linked to a positive tuberculin test, suggesting a possible relationship between growth parameters and latent tuberculosis infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1944
retrieved_docs.content: Endocervical biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on the cervix are both categorized as “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs.” This broader category of female‑organ diagnostic procedures is itself classified under the term منظار الرحم, indicating that these cervical diagnostic methods are considered part of the larger group of procedures performed with a uterine scope (hysteroscopy).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2708
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic endocrine procedures represent a broad category of diagnostic techniques that include الخزعة (biopsy). Within the scope of الخزعة, there are more specific child procedures, such as لطاخة المهبل (vaginal swab), which is a specialized form of biopsy targeting the vaginal mucosa. Recognizing that diagnostic endocrine procedures encompass الخزعة and that الخزعة has لطاخة المهبل as a sub‑procedure helps clinicians understand the hierarchical organization of these diagnostic methods and select the most appropriate technique for endocrine assessments and vaginal tissue sampling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2478
retrieved_docs.content: Endocervical biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on the cervix are both categorized as “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” a broad group of gynecologic diagnostic interventions. This same category also includes the procedure referred to in Arabic as “خزعة المهبل” (vaginal biopsy). By classifying endocervical biopsy, cervical diagnostic procedures, and vaginal biopsy under the umbrella of other diagnostic procedures for female organs, clinicians can better organize and understand the range of tissue‑sampling techniques used in female reproductive health, facilitating appropriate selection of diagnostic methods and consistent documentation in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853
retrieved_docs.content: Other diagnostic procedures on cervix are classified within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures; female organs. This overarching group of female‑organ diagnostic interventions includes procedures such as تنظيم جوف الحوض (pelvic endoscopy), خزعة المهبل (vaginal biopsy), and منظار الرحم (uterine hysteroscopy). Together, these procedures represent specialized diagnostic techniques used to evaluate the cervix, vagina, uterus, and surrounding pelvic structures in female patients, facilitating accurate assessment and management of gynecologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2477
retrieved_docs.content: Endocervical biopsy, open biopsy of the uterus, and other diagnostic procedures on the cervix are all classified as “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs.” This broader category of female‑organ diagnostic procedures is itself classified under منظار الرحم, the Arabic term for uterine endoscopy (hysteroscopy). Consequently, these specific biopsy techniques are considered part of the larger group of hysteroscopic diagnostic methods used in female reproductive health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1949
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic is a category that includes specific gynecologic evaluations such as Diagnostic procedures on ovaries and Diagnostic dilation and curettage of uterus. Both Diagnostic procedures on ovaries and Diagnostic dilation and curettage of uterus are classified as diagnostic procedures for the female genital system, indicating that they are specialized tests intended to assess ovarian and uterine pathology respectively. By grouping these procedures under the broader intent of Diagnostic, clinicians recognize them as essential tools for obtaining diagnostic information about the female reproductive organs.
